title: Travel Package Aggregation Service

summary.overview: This PR introduces a travel package aggregation service that compiles flight, hotel, and activity offerings from multiple supplier APIs into unified package bundles. The aggregation logic is well-designed, but the service suffers from N+1 query patterns in the pricing engine and lacks timeout configuration on external supplier API calls, posing reliability risks under load.

riskAssessment.summary: The service makes unbounded external API calls without timeout or circuit breaker protections. Combined with N+1 query patterns in the pricing engine, the service is vulnerable to cascading failures when supplier APIs degrade.
items: No timeout or circuit breaker configured on external supplier HTTP clients
N+1 query pattern in pricing engine causes linear database call growth per package component
No rate limiting on the aggregation endpoint to prevent upstream supplier throttling
Supplier API responses are not validated against expected schemas before processing
No fallback behavior when a single supplier is unavailable

strengths: Clean adapter pattern for supplier integration allows easy addition of new suppliers without modifying core aggregation logic
Comprehensive test suite covering aggregation scenarios including partial supplier failures and mixed currency handling
Well-defined domain model for travel packages with clear separation between supplier-specific and normalized representations
Currency conversion logic correctly handles rounding and exchange rate staleness with configurable refresh intervals

issues.message: The pricing calculation iterates over each package component and executes a separate database query to fetch supplier-specific pricing rules. For a package with 15 components, this results in 15 individual queries per pricing request.
issues.recommendation: Batch the pricing rule lookups into a single query using a WHERE IN clause with all component IDs, then map results in memory. Consider adding a short-lived cache for frequently accessed pricing rules.

issues.message: The HTTP client used for supplier API calls has no configured timeout, connection pool limits, or retry policy. A slow or unresponsive supplier will cause request threads to block indefinitely, eventually exhausting the connection pool.
issues.recommendation: Configure explicit connect and read timeouts (e.g., 3s connect, 10s read), implement a circuit breaker pattern using a library like opossum, and add retry logic with exponential backoff for transient failures.

issues.message: Supplier API calls are executed sequentially, resulting in aggregated latency equal to the sum of all supplier response times. A typical aggregation request touching three suppliers takes 2-4 seconds.
issues.recommendation: Execute independent supplier API calls concurrently using Promise.all with individual error handling per supplier, reducing total latency to the duration of the slowest supplier.

issues.message: Supplier API responses are directly mapped to domain objects without schema validation. Malformed or unexpected response shapes from suppliers will cause unhandled runtime exceptions.
issues.recommendation: Add response schema validation using a library like zod or joi before mapping supplier responses to domain objects. Log and gracefully handle validation failures with supplier-specific error context.

issues.message: Exchange rate cache uses a hardcoded 1-hour refresh interval with no jitter, causing all service instances to refresh simultaneously and spike load on the exchange rate provider.
issues.recommendation: Add a random jitter of 5-15 minutes to the refresh interval to distribute load across instances and prevent thundering herd effects on the rate provider.

recommendations: Configure timeouts, connection pool limits, and circuit breakers on all external supplier HTTP clients before production deployment
Refactor the pricing engine to use batch database queries, eliminating the N+1 pattern
Parallelize independent supplier API calls using Promise.all to reduce aggregation latency
Add response schema validation for all supplier API responses with structured error handling
Implement rate limiting on the aggregation endpoint to prevent upstream supplier throttling and protect service resources
Add a graceful degradation strategy that returns partial results when individual suppliers are unavailable rather than failing the entire request
Set up supplier health monitoring with automated circuit breaker alerts to the on-call team when a supplier error rate exceeds thresholds
